message:
feat(layout,parser,svg,wasm): add layout-aware source maps, expand DOT parser coverage, and fix SVG attribute handling

Replaces IR-only source maps with layout-enriched source maps that track
positioned elements including sequence diagram mirror headers. Expands DOT
parser to handle additional edge cases, adds integration tests for source
map fidelity, and updates SVG attribute rendering and WASM bindings.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-17e4.4","title":"Integrate proptest property testing for mathematical invariants","description":"## Proptest Property Testing for Mathematical Invariants

Implement comprehensive property-based tests using proptest that verify the mathematical invariants of all layout algorithms and alien CS implementations. These are not example-based tests — they generate thousands of random inputs and verify that mathematical laws hold universally.

## Invariants to Verify

**Layout algorithm invariants:**
1. **Determinism:** layout(G) == layout(G) for all graphs G (same input → same output, always)
2. **Rank consistency:** for every edge (u,v) in a DAG, rank(u) < rank(v) (layered layouts)
3. **Non-overlap:** for all node pairs (i,j), bounding_box(i) ∩ bounding_box(j) = ∅
4. **Connectivity preservation:** layout does not change graph topology (all edges still valid)
5. **Boundedness:** all coordinates within [0, MAX_COORD] (no Infinity, no NaN)

**CGA invariants (§12.11):**
6. **Rotor unitarity:** |R|² = R*R̃ = 1 for all rotors R
7. **Sandwich product idempotency:** R*(R*x*R̃)*R̃ = (R*R)*x*(R*R)̃ (composition law)
8. **Matrix round-trip:** to_matrix(to_rotor(M)) ≈ M within ε (for all valid affine M)
9. **Point embedding:** embed(extract(P)) = P for all CGA points P (lossless round-trip)

**E-graph invariants (§6.6):**
10. **Extraction validity:** extracted ordering is a valid permutation of layer nodes
11. **Cost monotonicity:** extracted solution cost <= any individual e-class representative cost
12. **Budget compliance:** e-graph size never exceeds configured max_enodes

**Lens invariants (§6.2):**
13. **GetPut:** put(s, get(s)) = s (modulo whitespace normalization)
14. **PutGet:** get(put(s, v)) = v (view edits round-trip)

## Implementation
1. Add proptest to fm-core, fm-layout, fm-parser dev-dependencies.
2. Implement `Arbitrary` for `Graph`, `NodeId`, `EdgeId`, `Rotor`, `Multivector`.
3. Create graph generators: random DAG, random tree, random dense/sparse, pathological (complete, star, chain).
4. Write property tests for all 14 invariants above.
5. Configure proptest: 10000 cases per property in CI, 1000 in local dev.
6. Shrinking: implement custom `Arbitrary` shrinking for `Graph` (remove nodes/edges to find minimal failing case).
7. Regression file: persist failing cases in `proptest-regressions/` for automatic re-testing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 14 invariants have corresponding proptest properties
- [ ] Custom Arbitrary implementations for Graph, Rotor, Multivector
- [ ] Graph generators cover: random DAG, tree, dense, sparse, pathological
- [ ] 10000 cases per property in CI (configurable)
- [ ] Custom shrinking produces minimal failing examples
- [ ] Regression files committed and re-tested automatically
- [ ] Zero property violations on current codebase","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:14.993885404Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T17:47:06.455536211Z","closed_at":"2026-03-19T17:47:06.455029301Z","close_reason":"Added 8 proptest properties for layout mathematical invariants: determinism (random DAG + star), rank consistency for Sugiyama DAGs, non-overlap for random DAGs, connectivity preservation, boundedness for all coords (random DAG + star + force-directed). 64 cases each = 512 random graphs per run. Graph generators: random_dag() and star_ir().","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["invariants","methodology","property-testing","proptest"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:14.993885404Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:42.022238936Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-17e4.5","title":"Implement CI quality gates for alien concept adoption","description":"## CI Quality Gates for Alien Concept Adoption

Implement CI pipeline gates that enforce the graveyard methodology: no alien concept integration merges without evidence, no performance regression without justification, no golden checksum changes without approval.

## Gate Definitions

**Gate 1: Golden Checksum Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout or fm-core
- Check: golden checksums unchanged, OR commit message contains [golden-update] with justification
- Fail action: block merge, link to golden checksum update workflow

**Gate 2: Performance Regression Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout, fm-core, or fm-parser
- Check: benchmark suite runs, all metrics within 10% of baseline (configurable threshold)
- Fail action: block merge if any metric regresses > 10%, warn if > 5%
- Exception: commit message contains [perf-regression-ok: reason]

**Gate 3: Property Test Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout, fm-core, fm-parser
- Check: all proptest properties pass with 10000 cases
- Fail action: block merge on any property violation

**Gate 4: Evidence Ledger Guard**
- Trigger: any bead with label "alien-cs" moved to complete status
- Check: corresponding evidence ledger entry exists with post-metrics
- Fail action: warn (not block), link to evidence creation workflow

**Gate 5: Determinism Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout
- Check: run layout 10 times on corpus, all outputs identical
- Fail action: block merge on non-determinism

**Gate 6: Decision Contract Guard**
- Trigger: any alien-cs epic moved to complete
- Check: decision contract document exists in evidence/ directory
- Fail action: warn (not block)

## Implementation
1. Define gates as GitHub Actions workflow steps (or equivalent CI).
2. Each gate is a separate job for parallel execution.
3. Gate results aggregated into a single merge-blocking status check.
4. Override mechanism: repo admin can force-merge with audit trail.
5. Gate configuration stored in `.ci/quality-gates.toml` (thresholds, exceptions).
6. Dashboard: CI generates a quality summary comment on each PR.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 6 gates implemented and running in CI
- [ ] Gates block merge on failure (except Gate 4 and 6 which warn)
- [ ] Override mechanism exists with audit trail
- [ ] Configuration is in-repo and version-controlled
- [ ] Quality summary comment posted on each PR
- [ ] Gate execution time < 10 minutes total (parallel execution)","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:30.166665414Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T16:15:03.209640830Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["automation","ci","methodology","quality-gates"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:30.166665414Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:42.133640448Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:42.245035750Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-17e4.6","title":"Create decision contract template and initial contracts for all alien concepts","description":"## Decision Contract Template and Initial Contracts

Create a formal decision contract template for alien CS concept adoption, and draft initial contracts for all alien concepts being considered for FrankenMermaid.

## Decision Contract Template
Each alien concept adoption must have a decision contract BEFORE implementation begins. The contract specifies:

```markdown
# Decision Contract: [Concept Name] (§X.Y)

## Graveyard Score: X.X / Tier: A|B|C

## Hypothesis
[What improvement do we expect? How much? On what workloads?]

## Acceptance Criteria (Adopt)
- [ ] Metric A improved by >= X% on benchmark corpus
- [ ] No metric regressed by > Y%
- [ ] Implementation complexity <= Z LOC
- [ ] Evidence ledger entry complete with measurements

## Rejection Criteria (Reject)
- [ ] Metric A improvement < X% (threshold for "not worth it")
- [ ] Any metric regression > Y%
- [ ] Implementation complexity > Z LOC
- [ ] Solver/algorithm timeout > T ms on target workload

## Evaluation Protocol
1. Baseline measurement (pre-implementation)
2. Implementation
3. Post-measurement (same corpus, same metrics)
4. Statistical comparison with confidence intervals
5. Decision: Adopt / Reject / Defer / Hybrid

## Timeline
- Baseline: [date]
- Implementation: [date range]
- Evaluation: [date]
- Decision: [date]

## Reviewers
[Who reviews and ratifies the decision?]
```

## Initial Contracts to Draft
1. **E-Graphs for Crossing Minimization (§6.6)** — Score 3.0, Tier B
   - Accept if: >= 15% crossing reduction on dense graphs, fallback < 100ms
   - Reject if: < 5% improvement or fallback > 500ms

2. **Swiss Tables for Node/Edge Maps (§7.7)** — Score 3.0, Tier B
   - Accept if: >= 20% lookup throughput improvement, >= 5% end-to-end
   - Reject if: < 5% end-to-end improvement (already using hashbrown)

3. **Conformal Geometric Algebra (§12.11)** — Score 2.5, Tier C
   - Accept if: code clarity measurably improved, performance within 2x of matrices
   - Reject if: performance > 3x of matrices on rendering hot path

4. **Constraint Programming for Layout (§9.7)** — Score 3.5, Tier B
   - Accept if: constraints respected, solve time < 1s for 200 nodes
   - Reject if: solve time > 5s for 100 nodes

5. **Bidirectional Lenses (§6.2)** — Score 4.0, Tier B
   - Accept if: GetPut and PutGet laws hold for 100% of corpus
   - Reject if: round-trip latency > 200ms on 500-node diagram

6. **Incremental Subgraph Re-Layout (§6.1)** — Score 6.7, Tier A
   - Mandatory adoption. Accept if: < 10ms update on 1000-node diagram
   - Remediation required if: > 50ms or non-deterministic

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Decision contract template created in evidence/TEMPLATE.md
- [ ] Initial contracts drafted for all 6 alien concepts above
- [ ] Each contract has specific, measurable accept/reject criteria
- [ ] Contracts reviewed and ratified before implementation begins
- [ ] Contracts version-controlled in evidence/contracts/ directory","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:48.635623903Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:41:13.427956875Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T01:41:13.427458842Z","close_reason":"Created decision contract template (evidence/TEMPLATE.md) and 6 initial contracts: E-Graphs (§6.6), Swiss Tables (§7.7), CGA (§12.11), Constraint Programming (§9.7), Bidirectional Lenses (§6.2), Incremental Re-Layout (§6.1). Each has specific measurable accept/reject criteria, evaluation protocols, and benchmark corpus definitions.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","decision-contract","documentation","methodology"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.6","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:48.635623903Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1a45","title":"C4: Implement technology labels, element icons, and legend rendering","description":"# C4 Diagram Full Rendering

## What mermaid-js Supports
C4 diagrams have rich element types with icons, descriptions, and technology labels:
- Person: stick figure icon
- System/Container/Component: rectangles with type label, name, technology, description
- External systems: dashed borders
- Databases: cylinder shape
- Queues: parallelogram shape
- Boundaries: labeled dashed rectangles
- Relationships: arrows with labels and technology annotations
- Legend: auto-generated showing element type → visual mapping
- LAYOUT_TOP_DOWN() / LAYOUT_LEFT_RIGHT() directives

## Current State
- All element types ARE parsed
- All relationship types ARE parsed
- Boundaries ARE parsed
- Technology/description fields within function args are NOT fully extracted (only first arg parsed)
- Element type icons NOT rendered
- Legend NOT rendered
- Layout directives parsed but NOT applied

## Implementation Plan
1. **Parser**: Parse all positional arguments in C4 function calls: (id, name, [technology], [description]). Store technology and description on IrNode.
2. **SVG Renderer**: Render element type labels (<<Person>>, <<System>>, etc.). Show technology in brackets below name. Show description in smaller text. Render person as stick figure. Render databases as cylinders. Render external elements with dashed borders.
3. **Layout**: Honor LAYOUT_TOP_DOWN/LEFT_RIGHT by setting graph direction.
4. **Legend**: Generate a legend box showing element type mapping.
5. **Tests**: Full C4 context diagram, container diagram, component diagram with all element types.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:47:12.054865942Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T18:39:12.289748896Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T18:39:12.289389887Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["c4","parity","parser","render"],"comments":[{"id":169,"issue_id":"bd-1a45","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Testing Contract (Mandatory)

This bead MUST ship with:

### Unit Tests
- Focused tests for every new parser path (positive + negative cases)
- Render output assertions (SVG element presence, attributes, structure)
- Edge cases: empty input, malformed input, boundary values

### Integration Tests
- Full pipeline: parse -> layout -> render for representative inputs
- Determinism check: same input -> identical output (3 runs)
- Graceful degradation: partially valid input produces best-effort output with diagnostics

### E2E Validation Script
Run via fm-cli with structured JSON logging. Required log fields: input_hash, diagram_type, node_count, edge_count, diagnostic_count, output_size_bytes, render_ms, pass_fail_reason.

### Quality Gates
All must pass before this bead can be closed:
- cargo check --workspace --all-targets
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings  
- cargo fmt --check
- cargo test --workspace (zero new failures)
","created_at":"2026-03-17T03:30:43Z"},{"id":189,"issue_id":"bd-1a45","author":"ubuntu","text":"SPECIFIC TEST CASES:
1. C4Context with Person, System, System_Ext → verify person stick figure icon, system rectangle, ext dashed border
2. Container with technology 'Java Spring' → verify technology renders in brackets below name
3. Rel with technology 'REST/HTTPS' → verify technology on relationship label
4. Container_Boundary with nested components → verify boundary dashed rectangle with title
5. LAYOUT_TOP_DOWN() → verify direction changes to TB
6. Legend → verify auto-generated legend box showing element type mapping
7. SystemDb → verify cylinder shape for database system
8. Full C4 deployment diagram with Deployment_Node nesting → verify nested boundaries","created_at":"2026-03-17T05:42:24Z"}]}

Must support paths, text, groups, transforms, clips, fills, strokes.","notes":"Added SVG scene-consumption slice for shared render IR: new fm-render-svg API render_scene_to_svg(scene, config) with recursive RenderGroup/RenderItem traversal, PathCmd->SVG path conversion (Move/Line/Cubic/Quad/Close), RenderTransform matrix emission, RenderClip defs registration + clip-path wiring, FillStyle/StrokeStyle/text align+baseline mapping, and source metadata attributes. Verified with cargo fmt --check, rch exec -- cargo test -p fm-render-svg, rch exec -- cargo check --workspace --all-targets, and rch exec -- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:59:23.295164265Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-28T03:43:14.413410265Z","closed_at":"2026-02-28T03:43:14.413391650Z","close_reason":"Completed target-agnostic render scene IR + initial SVG/Canvas scene consumers with tests and quality gates","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["frankenmermaid","rendering"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1br.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1br","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:22:47Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1t7l.4","title":"Property test suite verifying all lens laws (GetPut, PutGet, PutPut)","description":"## Lens Law Property Test Suite (§6.2 Phase 4)

Comprehensive property-based test suite using proptest that verifies all three lens laws hold for ParseLens, LayoutLens, and the composed DiagramLens across the full space of Mermaid diagrams and edit operations.

## Mathematical Foundation
**The three lens laws:**

1. **GetPut (round-trip):** `put(s, get(s)) = s`
   For any source s, parsing then unparsing recovers the original.
   This must hold EXACTLY for source text (modulo documented whitespace normalization).

2. **PutGet (edit reflection):** `get(put(s, v)) = v`
   For any source s and modified view v, the modification is faithfully reflected.
   This must hold for the positioned graph modulo layout re-optimization (documented tolerance).

3. **PutPut (edit composition):** `put(put(s, v1), v2) = put(s, v2)`
   Sequential edits compose correctly: the intermediate state does not affect the final result.
   This is the hardest law and may require documented exceptions for layout-dependent edits.

## Test Strategy

### ParseLens Laws
- **GetPut generators:** Random Mermaid source strings (via grammar-guided fuzzer or corpus sampling)
- **PutGet generators:** Random AST modifications (add node, remove node, rename, add edge, remove edge)
- **PutPut generators:** Pairs of AST modifications applied sequentially
- **Edge cases:** empty source, single-node, maximum-complexity, unicode labels, escaped characters

### LayoutLens Laws
- **GetPut generators:** Random graphs with random layout configs
- **PutGet generators:** Random position modifications (node drag, edge rerout)
- **Edge cases:** zero-node graph, disconnected components, self-loops, parallel edges

### DiagramLens Laws (composed)
- **GetPut:** Random Mermaid source -> parse -> layout -> compose put -> compare source
- **PutGet:** Random Mermaid source + random diagram edit -> put -> get -> compare diagram
- **PutPut:** Random Mermaid source + two random edits -> verify composition

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Implement `Arbitrary` for `MermaidSource` (grammar-guided generation via proptest strategies).
2. Implement `Arbitrary` for `AstEdit` (enum: AddNode, RemoveNode, RenameNode, AddEdge, RemoveEdge).
3. Implement `Arbitrary` for `DiagramEdit` (enum: MoveNode, AddEdge, DeleteEdge, ResizeNode).
4. Write proptest properties for all 3 laws x 3 lens levels = 9 property tests.
5. Configure: 10000 cases per property in CI, 1000 in local dev.
6. Custom shrinking: shrink source to minimal failing case, shrink edit to simplest operation.
7. Regression file: persist all failures in `proptest-regressions/lenses/` for re-testing.
8. Document acceptable deviations: whitespace normalization for GetPut, coordinate tolerance for PutGet.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 9 property tests: 3 laws x 3 lens levels (ParseLens, LayoutLens, DiagramLens)
- [ ] Custom Arbitrary implementations for MermaidSource, AstEdit, DiagramEdit
- [ ] 10000 test cases per property in CI
- [ ] Custom shrinking produces minimal failing examples
- [ ] All acceptable deviations documented with rationale
- [ ] Regression file committed and re-tested automatically
- [ ] Zero law violations on current implementation
- [ ] Coverage: all edit types exercised (add/remove/rename/move for nodes and edges)
- [ ] Logging: tracing::debug! on each law check, tracing::error! on violation with full context","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:10.452488410Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:23.210529741Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","lenses","proptest","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1t7l.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1t7l","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:10.452488410Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1t7l.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1t7l.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:23.210481280Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

Commands: detect, parse, render, validate, diff, capabilities. 10+ layout algorithms, best-effort parsing, deterministic output. 1040 tests pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["adoption-wedge","frankenmermaid"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1tsk","depends_on_id":"bd-1br","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:22:47Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1xma","title":"EPIC: E-Graphs for Crossing Minimization (§6.6)","description":"Use equality saturation (e-graphs) for optimal crossing minimization in Sugiyama layout. E-graphs represent ALL equivalent node orderings simultaneously, then extract the globally optimal ordering that minimizes edge crossings.

## Motivation
Current crossing minimization uses greedy barycenter heuristics (bd-vb9.5 improves these with sifting/transpose). E-graphs offer a fundamentally different approach: instead of exploring one ordering at a time, represent the entire equivalence class of valid orderings in a compact data structure, apply rewrite rules to explore the space, then extract the optimal solution. This can find globally optimal solutions that greedy heuristics miss.

## Approach — egg crate in Rust
- Use the egg (e-graphs good) crate, a mature Rust library for equality saturation.
- Encode each Sugiyama layer node ordering as an e-graph expression.
- Define rewrite rules: (a) swap adjacent nodes, (b) move node to position, (c) layer-local permutations.
- Cost function: total edge crossing count.
- Run equality saturation until convergence or budget exhaustion.

## Key Risks
- Performance unpredictability: e-graph saturation can explode for large layers. Mitigate with strict node budget (max e-class size) and wall-clock timeout.
- When budget/timeout exceeded, fall back to greedy Sugiyama (bd-vb9.5 heuristics).
- Quality may not always beat greedy: for simple graphs, overhead may not be justified. Use as optimization tier for complex/dense graphs only.

## Graveyard Reference: §6.6 — E-Graphs for Crossing Minimization
Score: 3.0 (Alien uplift). Risk: performance unpredictability.

## Success Metrics
- Crossing count reduction >= 15% vs greedy heuristics on dense test corpus.
- Fallback to greedy within 100ms wall clock for any graph.
- No OOM: memory bounded to 100MB for e-graph exploration.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:22:53.898378117Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:29:51.738073457Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","crossing-min","e-graph","layout"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1xma","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:29:51.738011591Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1xma","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:16.337345041Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-1xma.1","title":"Design E-graph encoding for node orderings in Sugiyama layers","description":"Design how Sugiyama layer node orderings are represented as e-graph expressions suitable for equality saturation.

## Deliverables
1. Define the e-graph language (egg Language trait): what are the expression nodes? Options: (a) Seq(node_id, rest) — linked-list encoding, (b) Perm(vec) — permutation encoding, (c) BinaryTree of swaps. Document tradeoffs of each.
2. Define rewrite rules that generate equivalent orderings: (a) adjacent swap, (b) block rotation, (c) median insertion.
3. Define the cost function: crossing_count(ordering_a, ordering_b_adjacent_layer) — must be efficiently computable during extraction.
4. Specify how multi-layer orderings interact: do we run per-layer or joint? Per-layer is simpler but may miss cross-layer optimizations.
5. Estimate e-graph size for typical inputs (10, 50, 100, 500 nodes per layer) and identify the practical scaling limit.

## Output
- Design doc with chosen encoding, rewrite rules, and cost function.

+{"id":"bd-1xma.1","title":"Design E-graph encoding for node orderings in Sugiyama layers","description":"Design how Sugiyama layer node orderings are represented as e-graph expressions suitable for equality saturation.

## Deliverables
1. Define the e-graph language (egg Language trait): what are the expression nodes? Options: (a) Seq(node_id, rest) — linked-list encoding, (b) Perm(vec) — permutation encoding, (c) BinaryTree of swaps. Document tradeoffs of each.
2. Define rewrite rules that generate equivalent orderings: (a) adjacent swap, (b) block rotation, (c) median insertion.
3. Define the cost function: crossing_count(ordering_a, ordering_b_adjacent_layer) — must be efficiently computable during extraction.
4. Specify how multi-layer orderings interact: do we run per-layer or joint? Per-layer is simpler but may miss cross-layer optimizations.
5. Estimate e-graph size for typical inputs (10, 50, 100, 500 nodes per layer) and identify the practical scaling limit.

## Output
- Design doc with chosen encoding, rewrite rules, and cost function.
- Prototype Rust types and egg Language impl (can be exploratory/throwaway).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:04.362702161Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-02T19:50:45.928565817Z","closed_at":"2026-04-02T19:50:45.928278603Z","close_reason":"Implemented e-graph ordering design and prototype in fm-layout/src/egraph_ordering.rs. Design doc embedded as module-level doc comments covering: (1) Encoding choice analysis (Seq vs Perm vs BinarySwap — chose Perm with adjacent swaps). (2) Rewrite rules: adjacent_swap, block_rotate, move_node (median insertion). (3) Cost function: crossing_count via merge-sort inversion counting O(n log n). (4) Multi-layer strategy: per-layer saturation with neighbor fixpoints. (5) Scaling estimates table (10-500 nodes/layer, practical limit ~100 nodes). Prototype types: LayerOrdering, LayerEdges, all rewrite operations, median_position, all_adjacent_swaps, estimate_egraph_size, should_use_egraph. 22 tests all passing. All quality gates pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","design","e-graph","layout"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:04.362702161Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1xma.2","title":"Implement equality saturation for crossing minimization","description":"Implement the core e-graph crossing minimization using the egg crate, based on the encoding design from bd-1xma.1.

## Scope
1. Add egg crate dependency to fm-layout Cargo.toml.
2. Implement the egg Language trait for the chosen node ordering representation.
3. Implement rewrite rules as egg Rewrite instances.
4. Implement the crossing count cost function as an egg CostFunction.
5. Build the integration point: given a Sugiyama layer assignment, construct the e-graph, run saturation, extract optimal ordering.
6. Wire into the Sugiyama pipeline as an alternative crossing minimization step (selectable via LayoutConfig).

## Testing
- Unit test: 3-node, 2-layer graph — e-graph finds optimal ordering.
- Unit test: known-optimal test case from crossing minimization literature.
- Integration test: e-graph ordering has fewer crossings than barycenter for dense test graphs.

## Dependencies
- egg crate (https://crates.io/crates/egg) — well-maintained, pure Rust.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:13.096633382Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:26.060232500Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","e-graph","implementation","layout"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:13.096633382Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:26.060181865Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1xma.3","title":"Implement node budget and timeout guards for E-graph saturation","description":"## Implement Node Budget and Timeout Guards for E-Graph Saturation (§6.6.3)

Implement runtime safety guards that prevent E-graph equality saturation from consuming unbounded memory or wall-clock time during crossing minimization. These guards are the critical safety layer that makes E-graph adoption production-viable.

## Mathematical Foundation
E-graph saturation has worst-case exponential growth. For a Sugiyama layer with n nodes, the permutation space is n!. The egg crate's `Runner` applies rewrite rules iteratively, growing the e-graph by merging equivalent expressions. Without guards, a 20-node layer could produce millions of e-nodes before convergence.

**Budget model (from §6.6 epic):**
- **Node budget:** `max_enodes = min(100_000, 50 * |V|^2)` where |V| is total graph node count
- **Time budget:** `T_max = min(500ms, 10ms * |layers|)` wall-clock per layer
- **Memory budget:** `M_max = 100MB` RSS delta for the e-graph arena
- **Iteration budget:** `max_iterations = 1000` rewrite rule applications

When ANY budget is exceeded, saturation halts immediately and signals the fallback path (bd-1xma.4).

## Key Papers
- Willsey et al., "egg: Fast and Extensible Equality Saturation" (POPL 2021) -- Runner::with_node_limit(), Runner::with_time_limit()
- Tate et al., "Equality Saturation: A New Approach to Optimization" (POPL 2009) -- termination conditions

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Create `EGraphBudget` struct in fm-layout with fields: `max_enodes: usize`, `max_time: Duration`, `max_memory_bytes: usize`, `max_iterations: usize`.
2. Implement `EGraphBudget::default_for_graph(node_count: usize, layer_count: usize)` using the formulas above.
3. Configure `egg::Runner` with `with_node_limit(budget.max_enodes)` and `with_time_limit(budget.max_time)`.
4. Implement custom `egg::ReportData` extension to track memory usage via a shim allocator (or RSS sampling at each iteration).
5. Implement custom `egg::IterationData` to record per-iteration metrics: e-node count, time elapsed, memory delta.
6. On budget exhaustion, the Runner stops and returns `StopReason::NodeLimit | StopReason::TimeLimit | StopReason::Other("memory")`.
7. Emit structured diagnostic: `EGraphBudgetExhausted { budget_type: BudgetType, value: u64, limit: u64, iterations_completed: u32, best_cost: f64 }`.
8. Wire budget configuration into `LayoutConfig` so users can tune thresholds.
9. Add `tracing::warn!` instrumentation at each budget check for observability.

## Rust Implementation Guidance
- Use `egg::Runner::with_node_limit()` and `egg::Runner::with_time_limit()` as primary guards.
- For memory tracking, wrap the global allocator with a counting shim (similar to `cap` crate pattern) scoped to the e-graph computation.
- Ensure all budget checks are branch-predicted (cold path) to minimize overhead on the hot saturation loop.
- Budget struct should implement `Clone + Debug + Serialize` for config/telemetry.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `EGraphBudget` struct with all four budget dimensions
- [ ] `default_for_graph()` computes budgets from graph properties
- [ ] egg Runner configured with node and time limits
- [ ] Memory tracking implemented (RSS sampling or allocator shim)
- [ ] Structured diagnostic emitted on every budget exhaustion
- [ ] Budget is configurable via `LayoutConfig`
- [ ] Unit tests: budget fires correctly at each threshold (node, time, memory, iteration)
- [ ] Property test: budget ALWAYS fires before OOM for graphs up to 1000 nodes
- [ ] Logging: `tracing::info!` on budget creation, `tracing::warn!` on exhaustion
- [ ] Benchmark: budget check overhead < 1% of saturation time","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:24.192428862Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:26.190309168Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","e-graph","layout","safety"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:24.192428862Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:26.190262190Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1xma.4","title":"Sugiyama fallback when E-graph exceeds budget","description":"## Sugiyama Fallback When E-Graph Exceeds Budget (§6.6.4)

When the E-graph equality saturation engine exceeds its node budget or wall-clock timeout during crossing minimization, the system must gracefully fall back to the proven greedy Sugiyama heuristics (barycenter + sifting from bd-vb9.5) without visible glitches or layout discontinuities.

## Mathematical Foundation
E-graph saturation has worst-case exponential growth: for a layer of n nodes, the number of possible orderings is n!, and the e-graph can grow to O(n! * R) e-nodes where R is the number of rewrite rule applications. The fallback mechanism implements a "anytime algorithm" pattern: at any interruption point, the best solution found so far is valid and usable.

**Budget Model:**
- Node budget: max_enodes = min(100_000, 50 * |V|^2) where |V| is total graph nodes
- Time budget: T_max = min(500ms, 10ms * |layers|)
- Memory budget: M_max = 100MB RSS delta for the e-graph arena
- If any budget exceeded: extract best-so-far from e-graph, compare crossing count with greedy result, return the better of the two.

## Key Papers
- Willsey et al., "egg: Fast and Extensible Equality Saturation" (POPL 2021) — extraction from partial saturation
- Gansner et al., "A Technique for Drawing Directed Graphs" (TSE 1993) — the Sugiyama baseline being fallen back to

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Wrap the `egg::Runner` with a custom `StopCondition` that checks node count, time, and memory at each iteration.
2. On budget exhaustion, call `egg::Extractor` with the crossing-count cost function to get the best ordering found so far.
3. In parallel, run the greedy barycenter heuristic on the same layer.
4. Compare crossing counts; pick the winner. Log which strategy won for telemetry.
5. Emit a structured diagnostic event: `LayoutDegradation::EGraphBudgetExceeded { budget_type, value, limit, fallback_strategy, crossing_delta }`.
6. Integrate with the global budget broker (bd-3uz.6) so E-graph budget counts against the layout stage allocation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Budget checks fire at correct thresholds (unit tests with synthetic large e-graphs)
- [ ] Fallback produces valid layout within 100ms wall clock for any graph
- [ ] Structured diagnostic emitted on every fallback invocation
- [ ] No panic, no OOM — property test with graphs up to 500 nodes, 2000 edges
- [ ] Crossing count of fallback result is <= greedy-only result (never worse than baseline)
- [ ] Integration with bd-3uz.6 global budget broker verified","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:35:46.501093013Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:40.989210880Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","crossing-min","e-graph","fallback"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:35:46.501093013Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1xma.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1xma.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:40.989142962Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-32su","title":"Spectral Graph Partitioning for Hierarchical Layout Decomposition","description":"## Spectral Graph Partitioning for Hierarchical Layout Decomposition

Use spectral graph partitioning (eigendecomposition of the graph Laplacian) to decompose large diagrams into well-separated subgraphs for hierarchical layout, enabling divide-and-conquer layout strategies that scale to thousands of nodes.

## Mathematical Foundation
The graph Laplacian L = D - A (degree matrix minus adjacency matrix) encodes the graph's connectivity structure. Its eigenvalues and eigenvectors reveal graph structure:
- λ_1 = 0 always (constant eigenvector)
- λ_2 (Fiedler value) = algebraic connectivity; its eigenvector (Fiedler vector) provides the optimal bisection
- λ_k reveals k-way partition quality

**Spectral bisection algorithm:**
1. Compute Fiedler vector v_2 (eigenvector of second-smallest eigenvalue of L)
2. Partition nodes: S_1 = {i : v_2[i] < median(v_2)}, S_2 = {i : v_2[i] >= median(v_2)}
3. This minimizes the normalized cut: NCut(S_1, S_2) = cut(S_1, S_2) * (1/vol(S_1) + 1/vol(S_2))
4. Recursively bisect for k-way partitioning

**For diagram layout:**
- Partition large diagram into 4-8 clusters
- Layout each cluster independently (parallel execution possible)
- Stitch clusters together using inter-cluster edge routing
- Result: O(n/k) layout per partition instead of O(n), with inter-cluster overhead

**Eigensolver choice:**
- For n < 500: dense eigendecomposition (O(n³))
- For n >= 500: Lanczos iteration (O(n*k*iter), typically iter < 100)
- Rust crate: nalgebra (dense) or sprs + arpack-like (sparse Lanczos)

## Key Papers
- Fiedler, "Algebraic Connectivity of Graphs" (Czech Math Journal 1973)
- Shi & Malik, "Normalized Cuts and Image Segmentation" (IEEE TPAMI 2000) — NCut
- Von Luxburg, "A Tutorial on Spectral Clustering" (Statistics and Computing 2007)
- Koren, "Drawing Graphs by Eigenvectors" (Computers & Mathematics 2005) — spectral layout

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Add `nalgebra` crate for eigendecomposition (already likely a transitive dependency).
2. Implement `SpectralPartitioner` that takes a graph and returns k partitions.
3. Compute Fiedler vector via Lanczos iteration for sparse graphs.
4. Implement recursive bisection for k-way partitioning.
5. Integrate with layout pipeline: for graphs > threshold (e.g., 200 nodes), spectral partition first, then layout each partition.
6. Implement inter-cluster edge routing (edges crossing partition boundaries).
7. Benchmark: layout time for 1000, 5000, 10000 node graphs with/without partitioning.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Spectral bisection produces balanced partitions (each partition within 40-60% of total)
- [ ] Fiedler vector computed correctly (verified against known graph spectra)
- [ ] Layout quality of partitioned approach within 10% of monolithic layout
- [ ] Layout time improvement >= 3x for 5000+ node graphs
- [ ] Inter-cluster edges routed without visual artifacts
- [ ] Threshold tuning: spectral partitioning skipped for small graphs (< 200 nodes)
- [ ] Decision contract: adopt if speedup >= 2x AND quality within 15%","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:46:53.071379760Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T20:48:00.183552929Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T20:48:00.183265365Z","close_reason":"Implemented spectral graph partitioning module (crates/fm-layout/src/spectral.rs) with: Laplacian construction from IR edges, Fiedler vector computation via nalgebra eigendecomposition, spectral bisection with balance constraints, recursive k-way partitioning, partition mapping for layout pipeline integration, threshold-based activation, normalized cut quality metric. 16 unit tests covering Laplacian properties, Fiedler vector correctness, barbell/clique/star/complete/path graph partitioning, balance constraints, partition mapping roundtrip, and quality metrics. All quality gates pass (check, clippy, fmt).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","graph-partitioning","layout","spectral"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-32su","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:09.332893165Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-32su","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:22:52.027515765Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-bium","title":"Implement flowchart parallel edge syntax (A & B --> C & D)","description":"# Flowchart Parallel Edge Syntax

## What mermaid-js Supports
\`\`\`
flowchart LR
  A & B --> C & D
\`\`\`
This creates 4 edges: A→C, A→D, B→C, B→D. The & operator allows declaring multiple source/target nodes in a single edge statement.

## Current State
- NOT parsed. The & character is not recognized in edge declarations.
- Mermaid parser's edge AST only supports single source → single target.

## Implementation Plan
1. **Parser**: In parse_edge_statement_asts(), before looking for arrow operators, split node references on '&'. If either side contains '&', expand to multiple FlowAst::Edge entries (cartesian product of sources × targets).
2. **Tests**: A & B --> C (2 edges), A --> B & C (2 edges), A & B --> C & D (4 edges), single node (no &, unchanged behavior).

## Considerations
- The & operator is only valid in flowchart, not other diagram types
- Must handle whitespace: 'A&B' vs 'A & B' vs 'A &B'","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-17T06:26:14.744584229Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-24T01:31:57.010578087Z","closed_at":"2026-03-24T01:31:57.010075205Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["flowchart","parity","parser"],"comments":[{"id":195,"issue_id":"bd-bium","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Testing Contract (Mandatory)

This bead MUST ship with:

### Unit Tests
- Focused tests for every new parser path (positive + negative cases)
- Render output assertions (SVG element presence, attributes, structure)
- Edge cases: empty input, malformed input, boundary values

### Integration Tests
- Full pipeline: parse -> layout -> render for representative inputs
- Determinism check: same input -> identical output (3 runs)
- Graceful degradation: partially valid input produces best-effort output with diagnostics

### E2E Validation Script
Run via fm-cli with structured JSON logging. Required log fields: input_hash, diagram_type, node_count, edge_count, diagnostic_count, output_size_bytes, render_ms, pass_fail_reason.

### Quality Gates
All must pass before this bead can be closed:
- cargo check --workspace --all-targets

 {"id":"bd-ckwu","title":"Sequence follow-up: layout/render participant group boxes from sequence_meta","description":"# Goal
Sequence `box ... end` groups are parsed into `IrSequenceMeta.participant_groups` but never laid out or rendered in the current pipeline, so the closed parity bead is not actually complete.

# Scope
- Convert `sequence_meta.participant_groups` into deterministic layout geometry in `fm-layout`.
- Render those group boxes across SVG, terminal, and canvas.
- Preserve deterministic ordering and nested-group behavior.
- Add focused tests proving `box` syntax produces visible output, not just parser metadata.

# Acceptance Criteria
- Sequence `box` groups appear in layout/render output across all three renderers.
- Geometry is deterministic and encloses the intended participant headers/lifelines.
- Focused parser/layout/render regression tests cover the feature.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-22T17:38:52.401186772Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T17:50:52.018035957Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T17:50:52.017538713Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["layout","parity","render","sequence"]}
 {"id":"bd-ct6f","title":"Sankey: Implement flow color gradients and value-proportional rendering","description":"# Sankey Rendering Improvements

## Gaps
- Flow bands should have color gradients (source color → target color)
- Flow width should be proportional to value (currently approximated)
- Node labels should be positioned outside the flow for readability
- Interactive hover highlighting (WASM only)

## Implementation Plan
1. Implement gradient fills on flow bands using linearGradient defs
2. Ensure flow band width exactly proportional to value/total_flow
3. Position node labels to the left of first-column nodes and right of last-column nodes
4. Add data attributes for WASM hover interaction","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:47:10.808759311Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T03:23:21.089716165Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T03:23:21.089436546Z","close_reason":"Added sankey-node CSS class to source/target nodes for styling.  {"id":"bd-drav","title":"Parse and render sequence diagram interaction fragments (loop/alt/opt/par/critical/break)","description":"# Sequence Interaction Fragments

## What mermaid-js Supports
\`\`\`
sequenceDiagram
  Alice->>Bob: Request
  loop Every minute
    Bob->>Alice: Heartbeat
  end
  alt Success
    Bob->>Alice: 200 OK
  else Failure
    Bob->>Alice: 500 Error
  end
  opt Optional
    Alice->>Carol: Forward
  end
  par Parallel
    Alice->>Bob: Task 1
  and
    Alice->>Carol: Task 2
  end
  critical Critical Section
    Alice->>Bob: Important
  option Timeout
    Alice->>Bob: Retry
  end
  break When error
    Alice->>Bob: Abort
  end
  rect rgb(200, 220, 240)
    Alice->>Bob: Highlighted
  end
\`\`\`

Fragment types: loop, alt/else, opt, par/and, critical/option, break, rect (colored background).
Each fragment is a labeled box spanning the involved participants, enclosing the messages within.
Fragments can be nested.

## Current State
- NOT parsed. Fragment keywords produce warnings.
- No layout support for fragment regions.
- No SVG rendering of fragment boxes.

## Implementation Plan
1. **Parser**: Add SequenceStatement::FragmentStart { kind, label }, FragmentElse { label }, FragmentEnd. Track nesting depth. Kind enum: Loop, Alt, Opt, Par, Critical, Break, Rect.
2. **IR**: Store as Vec<IrSequenceFragment> with kind, label, start_message_index, end_message_index, participant_range, and nested children.
3. **Layout**: Fragments add padding around their contained messages. Compute fragment box bounds from the first/last message y-positions and the leftmost/rightmost participant x-positions. Nested fragments get progressively larger bounds.
4. **SVG Renderer**: Draw fragment boxes as dashed-border rectangles with a label tab in the top-left corner. Fragment kind shown in bold in the tab. 'else'/'and'/'option' dividers shown as dashed horizontal lines within the fragment.
5. **Tests**: loop, alt/else, opt, par/and, nested fragments, rect with color.

## Complexity
This is the single most complex sequence diagram feature. Fragment nesting requires a stack-based parser and recursive layout. Budget 2-3x the effort of a simple task.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:44:09.719718813Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-18T02:25:34.360084926Z","closed_at":"2026-03-18T02:25:34.359593546Z","close_reason":"Implemented sequence interaction fragment parsing (loop, alt, opt, par, critical, break, rect). Stack-based parser tracks nesting depth. Fragments store kind, label, start/end edge indices, alternatives (for else/and/option dividers), and nested child indices. 'end' keyword dispatches to close either fragments or box groups. 6 tests added covering all fragment types, alt/else alternatives, nested fragments, rect with color, and warning-free parsing.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["layout","parity","parser","render","sequence"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-drav","depends_on_id":"bd-6zd9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:48:25.838352205Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-drav","depends_on_id":"bd-7g6d","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:48:29.797041627Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":146,"issue_id":"bd-drav","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Testing Contract (Mandatory)

This bead MUST ship with:

### Unit Tests
- Focused tests for every new parser path (positive + negative cases)
- Render output assertions (SVG element presence, attributes, structure)
- Edge cases: empty input, malformed input, boundary values

### Integration Tests
- Full pipeline: parse -> layout -> render for representative inputs
- Determinism check: same input -> identical output (3 runs)
- Graceful degradation: partially valid input produces best-effort output with diagnostics

### E2E Validation Script
Run via fm-cli with structured JSON logging. Required log fields: input_hash, diagram_type, node_count, edge_count, diagnostic_count, output_size_bytes, render_ms, pass_fail_reason.

### Quality Gates
All must pass before this bead can be closed:
- cargo check --workspace --all-targets

","created_at":"2026-03-17T03:30:40Z"},{"id":175,"issue_id":"bd-drav","author":"ubuntu","text":"SCOPE NOTE: This bead is intentionally large because the fragment parser, layout integration, and renderer are tightly coupled. Splitting parser from renderer would create a half-baked intermediate state (parsed but invisible fragments). Implementors should budget 2-3x normal effort. Suggested approach: implement loop first (simplest fragment), then alt/else (adds alternation), then the rest. Each fragment type can be tested incrementally.

DETAILED TEST CASES:
1. Simple loop: loop Every minute / Bob->>Alice: ping / end → verify dashed box with 'loop' tab, contains 1 message
2. Alt with else: alt OK / A->>B: 200 / else Error / A->>B: 500 / end → verify two sections separated by dashed line
3. Nested: loop / alt / A->>B: x / else / A->>B: y / end / end → verify nested boxes with correct containment
4. Par with and: par / A->>B: t1 / and / A->>C: t2 / end → verify parallel sections
5. Rect with color: rect rgb(200,200,240) / A->>B: x / end → verify colored background
6. Empty fragment: loop Empty / end → verify empty box renders without crash
7. Fragment spanning all participants: verify box width covers leftmost to rightmost participant involved
8. 3-level nesting: loop / critical / opt / A->>B / end / end / end → verify 3 nested boxes","created_at":"2026-03-17T05:41:30Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-dsjl","title":"Sequence: implement rect background highlighting parity","description":"# Sequence rect highlighting parity

## Goal
Make Mermaid sequence `rect COLOR ... end` fragments render as actual background highlighting instead of a generic fragment box.

## Scope
- Preserve the rect color distinctly in sequence IR and layout extensions.
- Render SVG rect fragments with the configured fill color and a sensible border, while keeping unsafe paint values out of the output.
- Keep non-rect fragments on existing generic fragment rendering.
- Add focused parser/layout/render tests.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `rect rgb(...)` and `rect rgba(...)` render as highlighted sequence regions in SVG.
- `rect transparent` renders without an opaque fill.
- Existing loop/alt/opt/par/critical/break fragment rendering remains intact.
- Required cargo checks pass via `rch` and formatting is clean.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-23T21:15:29.112583706Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T21:19:40.040611440Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T21:19:40.040101485Z","close_reason":"Implemented sequence rect background highlighting parity","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
